text.heading: Nigeria to US: We are not at war in Niger Delta
text.p: The military is not at war with the people of Niger Delta, the Federal Government said yesterday.
Explaining that the ongoing operation in the region is to flush out the "criminal elements", the government it would not be provoked to declare war on its own citizens.
It identified illegal oil bunkerers and arms dealers as those behind the restiveness in the region.

Minister of Niger Delta Affairs, Ufot Ekaette, and his Foreign Affairs counterpart, Ojo Maduekwe, made these known in Washington, United States (US) at a special session organised by the Centre for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS).

Ekaette, who spoke on how to resolve the Niger Delta crisis and bring about peace and development, said the armed forces were not at war with the Niger Delta and would never be despite provocation by criminals posing as Niger Delta crusaders.

A statement by the Special Assistant (Media) to Ekaette quoted him as saying that contrary to the propaganda by the oil bunkerers and arms dealers, the government was doing its best to checkmate criminal tendencies in the Niger Delta to pave the way for genuine peace and development.

The statement reads in part: "Ekaette said that it was wrong for the same people who accused the government of not developing the Niger Delta to turn round and drive away development agents, including contractors and foreign oil workers through kidnapping for ransom, killing and destruction of facilities."

Ekaette, who pointed out that it was in a bid to significantly improve the Niger Delta that the Federal Government created the Ministry of Niger Delta, added that the government has already worked out a concrete development programme for the region.
The ministry said it had worked out modalities for the construction of 45 new intra-and-inter-city roads in addition to the multi-billion Niger Delta Coastal and East-West roads.

The statement added: "He also said that no fewer than 360 housing units had been slated for construction in the nine states that make up the Niger Delta by the new ministry, while the training of youths will begin next month.
"Ekaette also said an inventory of dumpsites and oil impacted sites in the Niger Delta region was being undertaken with a view to remedying the areas for productive uses by the communities.

"He, however, appealed to the international community to support Nigeria’s effort to end the Niger Delta crisis by checking the sale of illegal of arms into the Niger Delta in exchange for Nigeria’s stolen crude oil by criminals.

"The minister stated that the Federal Government was determined to end the crisis by offering amnesty to those willing to lay down their arms and be trained and re-integrated into the society for meaningful life.

"Minister of Foreign Affairs, Chief Ojo Maduekwe, made it clear that the recent crisis in the Niger Delta was triggered by criminals who ambushed and killed Nigerian soldiers on routine duties and said that the soldiers were not out to engage in wanton destruction of lives and property of any community.

"Maduekwe drew the attention of the audience to the fact that as a country that emerged from a very painful civil war, Nigeria would not allow any group or individual to plunge it into avoidable conflict and appealed to the international community to allow Nigeria to take steps to resolve the problems posed by criminals in the Niger Delta."
